reference_answer: 26-574-ATL. Stated at the bottom of the release under "Release Number:".
April 1, 2026.
Lori Chavez-DeRemer, U.S. Secretary of Labor.
BOSTON. The dateline appears at the start of the lead paragraph and is repeated.
$4,699,362 in proposed penalties (also expressed as "$4.6M" in the release's subhead/tagline).

- "Please note: Information in some news releases may be out of date or not reflect current policies." - "Notice: Information in some news releases may not reflect the current or final status of a case, including, but not limited to, the penalty amount(s) or classification(s)." The second is significant: it explicitly warns that the penalty amount and the classifications (willful/repeat/serious) cited in the release may be subject to change.
"OSHA's National Emphasis Program on Trenching and Excavation." Referenced in the penultimate paragraph before the contact info.
1. Eric R. Lucero — 678-237-0630, lucero.eric.r@dol.gov 2. Erika B. Ruthman — 678-237-0631, ruthman.erika.b@dol.gov 3. Juan J. Rodríguez — 972-850-4709, rodriguez.juan@dol.gov
Three classifications: - Willful: 7 citations - Repeat: 33 citations - Serious: 17 citations

All three counts appear in a single sentence: "The agency cited the employer with seven willful citations, 33 repeat, and 17 serious violations for:" — followed by a single bulleted list of hazard categories. The counts are *not* themselves rendered as a list. The headline rephrases the same information ("7 willful, 33 repeat") but omits the serious count.

1. Failing to provide workers with a safe way to exit the trench. 2. Lack of adequate cave-in protection. 3. Having unsupported underground utilities. 4. Maintaining spoil piles within two feet of an excavation. 5. Neglecting to install a shoring system per the design. 6. Using a damaged protective system. 7. Exposing employees to numerous electrical and fall hazards.
- Incident date: Nov. 18, 2025 (also phrased as "November 2025" in the lead). - Release date: April 1, 2026.
7 + 33 + 17 = 57 total citations.

$4,699,362 rounds to $4.7M when rounded to the nearest $0.1 million (since $4,699,362 / $1,000,000 = 4.699362, which rounds up to 4.7). The release's tagline "$4.6M" is therefore an under-rounding — strictly, the figure is closer to $4.7M than to $4.6M. Several secondary news outlets reporting on this same release used "$4.7 million." The internal inconsistency is a feature of the source.
Incident: Nov. 18, 2025. Release: April 1, 2026. Elapsed: 134 days (approximately 4 months, 14 days).

15 business days from receipt of the citations and penalties. Three options: 1. Comply with the citations and pay penalties. 2. Request an informal conference with OSHA's area director. 3. Contest the findings before the independent Occupational Safety and Health Review Commission.
- One worker was engulfed and sustained fatal injuries. - A second worker was seriously injured. (Both were trapped inside the trench.)
Approximately 4 months and 14 days. This is below OSHA's six-month statutory deadline for issuing citations following an inspection (29 U.S.C. § 658(c)).
Same as Q16: 1. Comply with the citations. 2. Request an informal conference with OSHA's area director. 3. Contest the findings before the Occupational Safety and Health Review Commission.
Workers were removing sandy soil and installing steel plates outside of the trench. Backfilled sand collapsed (gave way) into the trench, trapping two workers.

A shoring system. Bullet #5 in the cited-hazards list reads: "Neglecting to install a shoring system per the design." The phrase "per the design" indicates the system was specified in the project design but not installed.

- Independent body for contesting: "the independent Occupational Safety and Health Review Commission" (OSHRC). - For informal conferences: "OSHA's area director" (no individual named). The release identifies two different procedural paths involving two different bodies — one inside OSHA (the area director) and one independent of OSHA (OSHRC).

Two workers were trapped inside the trench. One was engulfed by the collapsing sand and sustained fatal injuries; the other was seriously injured but survived. (The release does not name either worker; secondary reporting identifies the deceased worker, but the OSHA release itself does not.)

(a) "OSHA's National Emphasis Program on Trenching and Excavation." (b) Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer is quoted saying: "This cave-in is a solemn reminder of the dangers construction workers face when basic safety procedures and safe engineering solutions are ignored. Through our trench safety initiatives, the Department of Labor remains committed to ensuring every worker returns home safe at the end of the day…We will continue holding employers accountable and providing resources dedicated to hazard training and required engineering controls to put a stop to these preventable tragedies." The quote ties the specific cited violations (cave-in protection, shoring system) to DOL's ongoing trench-safety policy. The phrase "required engineering controls" maps directly to bullet #5 ("shoring system per the design").

The text does not say so explicitly. The seven bullets are framed as the *subjects* of the combined willful + repeat + serious citations ("cited the employer with seven willful citations, 33 repeat, and 17 serious violations **for:**" followed by the bullets). With 57 total citations spread across 7 categories, the bullets must be hazard *categories*, not 1:1 with willful citations. The numerical coincidence (7 bullets, 7 willful) is misleading; the answer to the question is **no, the correspondence is not one-to-one**, and the release does not assign specific bullets to specific willful/repeat/serious classifications. A qualified, uncertainty-acknowledging answer is the correct one.
